title: Evaluation of chili pepper varieties for resistance to Alternaria alternata: Growth, biochemical, and post-harvest responses.
author_string: Roquyya, Ghulam, Shoaib, Amna, Perveen, Shagufta, Rafiq, Muhammad
abstract: Alternaria alternata is a major fungal pathogen affecting chili pepper production, causing severe pre- and post-harvest losses and significant yield reductions worldwide. This study aimed to evaluate the resistance of chili pepper varieties to A. alternata using foliar, soil, and detached leaf assays as standardized screening tools, and to assess their growth, biochemical, and post-harvest responses. Six chili pepper varieties (Golden Hot, SV 1678, Shehzadi, gsl 2002, 777, and Super Green) were subjected to foliar and soil inoculation. Overall, foliar inoculation resulted in significantly higher disease incidence and severity compared to soil inoculation. In susceptible varieties (Super Green, 777, and gsl 2002), foliar inoculation produced disease incidence of 63-80 % and severity of 51-84 %, whereas soil inoculation resulted in comparatively lower incidence (35-52 %) and severity (28-46 %). Resistant varieties (Golden Hot, SV 1678, and Shehzadi) exhibited much lower disease under both treatments, with foliar inoculation causing 5-15 % incidence and 4-10 % severity, while soil inoculation showed only 2-8 % incidence and 1-6 % severity. Shoot and root growth were significantly suppressed in susceptible varieties, particularly under foliar inoculation, while resistant varieties showed minimal growth reductions under both treatments. Foliar inoculation induced higher activity of defense enzymes (catalase: CAT, peroxidase: POX, polyphenol oxidase: PPO, and phenylalanine ammonia-lyase: PAL) in resistant varieties compared to soil inoculation. In the detached leaf assay, resistant cultivars had minimal lesion growth, while susceptible varieties exhibited larger lesions. Post-harvest resistance was quantified using a 1-9 degradation scale, with higher scores in susceptible varieties reflecting greater tissue softening and decay. These results highlight the varying resistance of chili pepper varieties to A. alternata and underscore the importance of inoculation method in evaluating disease progression.
